coredata.dc:title: An efficient FRET based theranostic nanoprobe for hyaluronidase detection and cancer therapy in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yazhou
Zhao, Hengzhi
Gao, Na
Yang, Chunlei
Zhang, Rubo
Zhang, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: Although various strategies have been developed in cancer theranostics, it’s still a great challenge to achieve a sensitive diagnosis and specific treatment of cancer. Herein, a theranostic nanoprobe (PDAD@F68-HA-DOX) has been successfully fabricated through self-assembly. Under the stimulus of hyaluronidase (HAase), ratiometric fluorescent feedbacks would be received through this FRET-based nanoprobe, exhibiting predominant selectivity and sensitivity. Compared with other analytical approaches, this FRET-based probe with suitable detection limit and linear range could be favorable for the determination of target enzyme. Moreover, it is also able to differentiate tumor cells from normal cells by labeling them with different fluorescence colors. More importantly, this theranostic system could be activated to accomplish specific release of chemotherapeutic drug in lesion areas, resulting in discriminative cytotoxicity to tumor and normal cells. Apparently, this versatile nanoprobe has exhibited great potentials in the early diagnosis and treatment of cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FRET
Theranostic nanoprobe
Hyaluronidase detection
Cancer therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The construction of FRET system for HAase detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 21275018 and 21575015 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The formation of the theranostic nanoprobe (PDAD@F68-HA-DOX) and its behavior in tumor cells.
Photographs of EDC (left), DA (middle), and PDAD (right) solutions (A) with or (B) without UV irradiation. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectra of EDC, DA, and PDAD solutions.
TEM images of (A) PDADs, (B) the local enlarged image of (A), (C) F68-HA-DOX, and (D) PDAD@F68-HA-DOX. Scale bars are 10 nm in (A), 100 nm in (C, D).
(A) Size distributions and (B) zeta potentials of PDADs, F68-HA-DOX, and PDAD@F68-HA-DOX. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of PDADs, F68, HA, DOX, and PDAD@F68-HA-DOX.

(A) Photographs and (B) fluorescence spectra of PDAD solutions, with different amounts of HA-DOX added. (C) The relationship between the fluorescence emission spectrum of PDADs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX. (D) Photographs and (E) fluorescence spectra of nanoprobe solutions containing different levels of HAase. (F) Linear relationship between the fluorescence ratio (I475/I600) and the HAase concentrations.

(A) Confocal fluorescence microscopy images and (B) fluorescence ratio (Iblue/Ired) of HeLa cells, HeLa cells pretreated with HA, and NIH-3T3 cells after the addition of PDAD@F68-HA-DOX into culture medium.
Cytotoxicity caused by various concentrations of PDAD@F68-HA, PDAD@F68-HA-DOX, and DOX to (A) HeLa cells and (B) NIH-3T3 cells. The horizontal axis is the concentration of DOX in different NPs.

head.ce:title: An efficient FRET based theranostic nanoprobe for hyaluronidase detection and cancer therapy in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Cluster Science of Ministry of Education, Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Cluster Science of Ministry of Education
Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Cluster Science of Ministry of Education, Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient FRET based theranostic nanoprobe has been contructed successfully for hyaluronidase detection and cancer therapy.

Although various strategies have been developed in cancer theranostics, it’s still a great challenge to achieve a sensitive diagnosis and specific treatment of cancer. Herein, a theranostic nanoprobe (PDAD@F68-HA-DOX) has been successfully fabricated through self-assembly. Under the stimulus of hyaluronidase (HAase), ratiometric fluorescent feedbacks would be received through this FRET-based nanoprobe, exhibiting predominant selectivity and sensitivity. Compared with other analytical approaches, this FRET-based probe with suitable detection limit and linear range could be favorable for the determination of target enzyme. Moreover, it is also able to differentiate tumor cells from normal cells by labeling them with different fluorescence colors. More importantly, this theranostic system could be activated to accomplish specific release of chemotherapeutic drug in lesion areas, resulting in discriminative cytotoxicity to tumor and normal cells. Apparently, this versatile nanoprobe has exhibited great potentials in the early diagnosis and treatment of cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has become one of the most serious threats to humans’ lives and health, mainly due to the reason it is usually diagnosed at advanced stage [1–3]. To date, early diagnosis and treatment of cancer are still the most effective way to improve the survival rate of patients [3–6]. Based on the fact that tumor cells are usually accompanied with various abnormal expressed physiological components (such as biomolecules, enzymes), diverse smart systems for cancer theranostics have been developed [7–9].

Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a kind of bio-polysaccharide with repetitive disaccharide groups composed of glycuronic acid and 2-acetamido-2-deoxy-d-glucose [10,11]. It is widely distributed in the extracellular matrix and synovial fluid of vertebrates, and could participate in many physiological processes (involving cell movement, proliferation, and differentiation) [12]. There is a high affinity between HA and the overexpressed CD-44 receptors on the tumor cell surface [13]. After the specific recognition, HA can enter cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis, and then be decomposed into oligomeric fragments under the action of intracellular hyaluronidase (HAase) [14,15]. HAase, a glycoside hydrolase, is up-regulated and closely related to many physiological processes in malignant tumors [10,16,17]. The expressed level of this biological enzyme in tumor cells can provide a large amount of information about cancer at different stages, so the detection of intracellular HAase is very significant for the early diagnosis and subsequent treatment of cancer.

Up to now, researchers have developed lots of strategies to detect HAase, such as turbidity, viscosity, colorimetry, immunoassay, and so on [18–22]. However, these methods often have many drawbacks, such as complex testing process, long time consuming, expensive testing instruments, and insufficient sensitivity. In particular, few of them could be employed to investigate the HAase level in the organism. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and reliable approach to detect endogenous HAase. It is widely known that fluorescent technique is a valuable analytical method and has unique advantages in bioimaging and bioassay: noninvasive detection, rapid response, high sensitivity, and et al. [23–25]. Nowadays, some fluorescent nanoprobes have been developed for HAase detection [26,27]. For example, HA were utilized as linkers to connect gold nanoparticles (NPs) and luminescent materials, and the presence of adjacent gold NPs would quench the fluorescence of luminescent materials [28–30]. Intracellular HAase could remove the quenching effect through the hydrolysis of HA, and ultimately the activity of HAase can be determinated by detecting the recovered fluorescence. However, this off-on mechanism could only provide single-wavelength fluorescence changes, whose accuracy would be largely affected by the external interferences. Meanwhile, since HAase is overexpressed in tumor cells, it might be promising that these nanoprobes possess the capacity to in situ damage those infinitely proliferating cells. Recently, our group reported a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system based on carbon dots and anticancer drugs, which had demonstrated its advance in HAase detection and cancer treatment [31]. However, the overlap between fluorescence emission spectrum of donors and UV–vis absorption spectrum of receptors is relatively little, leading to the detection limit and linear range unsuitable for the intracellular assay. In addition, electrostatic interaction between FRET-paired fluorophores with opposite charges would assemble them together, even if HA has been decomposed, which could interfere with the detection accuracy. Therefore, an effective theranostic nanoprobe should be expected to own the following properties: (1) a highly efficient FRET system, (2) a ratiometric probe for accurate HAase detection, (3) an effective anticancer drug.

Polydopamine NPs are derived from dopamine (DA), and those with the diameter of greater than 50 nm usually have an excellent photothermal conversion property and have been extensively used in antimicrobial and anticancer experiments [32,33]. Recently, the existence of fluorescent polydopamine was reported with a high fluorescence quantum yield. Moreover, these NPs also present a good biocompatibility, and had been applied in some biochemical tests [34–37]. As a widely used chemotherapeutic drug in clinical trials, doxorubicin (DOX) can not only cause damage to many kinds of tumor cells, but also emit steady fluorescence under suitable excitation light [25]. A zero-dimensional fluorescent polydopamine quantum dots (PDADs) were obtained by one-step synthesis, and its fluorescence emission spectrum could perfectly match the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX. The spectral properties of PDADs and DOX provide a basis to build an efficient FRET system. Meanwhile, pluronic F68 (F68), a polymer approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), was incorporated into this drug delivery system to strengthen its stability and prevent drug leakage.

Herein, we construct an efficient theranostic nanoprobe (PDAD@F68-HA-DOX) to detect the endogenous HAase and in situ induce the tumor cell apoptosis. As exhibited in Fig. 1 , these nanoprobes could emit red fluorescence of DOX under the irradiation of excitation light of PDADs (405 nm) through the FRET process. However, after the internalization of nanodrugs by tumor cells, HA would be disintegrated by the overexpressed HAase in the tumor microenvironment. Without the constraint of HA, FRET-paired fluorophores with similar positive changes would repel each other, resulting in the collapse of nano-framework and the release of loaded chemotherapeutic drugs (DOX). The released DOX could remove the quenching effect on the fluorescent quantum dots, so blue fluorescence of PDADs would be recovered under appropriate irradiation. Based on the fluorescence feedbacks derived from the hydrolytic action of HAase, the determination of HAase could be realized and further largely contribute to the discrimination between normal and tumor cells. Simultaneously, the released DOX would effectively damage tumor cells, while there are little side effects on normal cells. Therefore, this theranostic nanoprobe, which is able to label different fluorescence colors and induce discriminative cytotoxicity on normal and tumor cells, would play a crucial role in the early diagnosis and treatment of cancer.

1-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC∙HCl), Dopamine hydrochloride (DA∙HCl), F68, HA (Mw = 5000), doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX∙HCl), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), HAase, bovine serum albumin (BSA), glucose, glutathione (GSH), and cysteine were purchased from manufactures, and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100, JEOL) was employed to confirm the morphology of NPs. Mastersizer (Nano-ZS 90, Malvern Instrument) was used to evaluate the size distribution and zeta potential. Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were investigated by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi) and a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1901, Persee), respectively. And confocal images of cells could be achieved through a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (FV1000, Olympus). The fluorescence quantum yield was measured by a steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000).

PDADs were synthesized through one-step approach, which could be briefly described as below. Firstly, DA (40 mM) and EDC (50 mM) reserved solutions were prepared through the additions of DA∙HCl and EDC∙HCl into PBS solutions (pH = 7.4), respectively. Subsequently, 2 mL of DA solution was added in to 2 mL of EDC solution at room temperature (25 °C) under vigorous stirring for 3 h. After that, non-fluorescent NPs with large particle size were removed from the olive-green solution through centrifugation (10,000 rpm ×15 min), and then the unreacted reactants were purified with dialysis bag (1 kDa).

Based on previous report, HA-DOX was prepared by dehydration condensation of carboxyl groups of HA and amino groups of DOX [31]. Before the introduction of DOX, the carboxyl groups of the HA should be activated. Briefly, EDC∙HCl (20 mg) and NHS (15 mg) were added into HA solution (5 mg/mL ×10 mL) and reacted for 2 h under stirring conditions. Subsequently, 5 mg of DOX∙HCl was added into the activated HA solution and kept stirring for overnight. After three days of dialysis (3.5 kDa), the purified HA-DOX was received by freeze-drying the reactive solution.

To fabricate PDAD@F68-HA-DOX nanoprobe, F68 and HA-DOX were mixed to assemble F68-HA-DOX NPs firstly. Briefly, 4 mg of purified HA-DOX was added into F68 solution (5 mg/mL ×2 mL) and kept stirring for 4 h. After that, the prepared PDADs were loaded onto the surface of the preassembled F68-HA-DOX NPs by electrostatic interaction, and purified PDAD@F68-HA-DOX could be obtained after dialysis (10 kDa).

The loading efficiency of different components could be quantified according to the following formula: LC (%) = (total drug – free drug)/total drug × 100. Free DOX and PDADs were measured by their UV–vis absorption spectra. In detail, different concentrations of DOX and PDADs solutions were configured, and then the standard curves were plotted based on their characteristic absorbance peaks. Based on the contents of unloaded DOX and PDADs, the loading efficiencies of DOX and PDADs in the nanodrugs were calculated, respectively.

First, HAase (200 U/mL) and PDAD@F68-HA-DOX NPs (1 mg/mL) was dissolved in PBS solution (pH = 5.6), respectively. Subsequently, different volumes of HAase reserved solutions were added into 1 mL of PDAD@F68-HA-DOX solutions. After 120 min of incubation at 37 °C, these mixed solutions were diluted to 6 mL and then used to investigate their fluorescence spectra with laser excitation at 405 nm. To analyze the selectivity of nanoprobe, various potential competitive substances were added into nanoprobe solutions, mainly including: NaCl, KCl, MgSO4, ascorbic acid, glucose, GSH, cysteine, BSA, ALP, lysozyme, trypsin, and HAase, and subsequently the fluorescence emission spectra were measured.

Cervical cancer (HeLa) and mouse embryonic fibroblasts (NIH-3T3) cells were incubated into Dulbecco's modified eagle medium with 10 % FBS and 1% dual antibody, then placed in incubator containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

The toxicity caused by the nanodrugs was estimated through MTT method [38]. 100 μL of HeLa and NIH-3T3 cells were seeded into 96-well plates with cell density of about 1 × 104 cells per well. After 24 h of culture, the medium in each well was removed, and 200 μL of medium solutions containing different concentrations of nanodrugs were added and then placed in an incubator for further culture. After one day, MTT reserved solutions were added into every well at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL and continue 4 h of incubation. Finally, the medium solutions in the wells were removed and 150 μL of DMSO solutions were added. After 10 min of vibration, the absorbance of each well was estimated by a microplate reader at the wavelength of 570 nm.

CLSM was utilized to monitor the release of chemotherapeutic drugs in real time. First, two kinds of cells were seeded into confocal microplates. After 24 h of culture in the incubator, different concentrations of various drugs were added into the medium and then placed in the incubator for further culture. Finally, the medium was replaced with new medium at the predeterminate time points, and the cells were monitored through CLSM.

Herein, PDADs were synthesized through mixing EDC and DA solutions, whose reaction mechanism was illustrated in Fig. S1 [39]. After combination, the initial transparent reaction solutions became olive-green and could emit intense blue fluorescence under the exposure of UV light (Fig. 2 A, B). Their UV–vis absorption spectra were also investigated, shown in Fig. 2C. Two obvious characteristic peaks can be observed in the absorption spectrum of PDAD solution: a strong absorption peak at about 295 nm corresponded to phenolic hydroxyl group, and a wide absorption peak in the range of 350−460 nm assigned to PDADs, suggesting the generation of PDADs. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra of PDADs were scanned to study their fluorescence property with excitation light at different wavelengths. As displayed in Fig. S2, PDAD solutions exhibit the maximal fluorescence emission at 475 nm, which hardly shift with the excitation wavelength, and the optimal excitation wavelength was at 415 nm. This phenomenon that maximal emission peak is independent of the excitation wavelength is distinctly different from conventional quantum dots, which may be caused by the homogeneous content of prepared PDADs. These PDADs containing single-photon emitters are highly favorable for bioimaging and quantitative analysis [28].

The morphology of PDADs was investigated through TEM, as exhibited in Fig. 3 A. PDADs are uniformly dispersed spherical NPs with a diameter of about 7 nm, which is consistent with their size distribution results (Fig. 4 A). Remarkably, it is the extremely small particle size (< 10 nm) of PDADs that induces their Fermi energy level to split from the continuous state, resulting in their energy level transition and fluorescent emission under appropriate irradiation. Meanwhile, lattice could be clearly observed from the enlarged TEM image (Fig. 3B). It might be due to the fact that the strong π-π stacking force between the layers caused by the aggregation of PDADs could urge them to arrange in a regular and orderly manner and ultimately generate the crystal structure. In order to further study the chemical structure of fluorescent PDADs, infrared spectra were also measured to ascertain the presence of various functional groups. As shown in Fig. S3, the strong stretching peak at 3440 cm−1 could be assigned to OH vibration, the peaks at 1635 and 1395 cm−1 are relative to the bending vibration of NH and CH bonds, and the stretching peak at 1156 and 1084 cm−1 may be caused by CO and CN vibration.

Afterward, F68-HA-DOX and PDAD@F68-HA-DOX NPs were fabricated through assembly, and their synthetic route was briefly exhibited in Fig. 1. The morphology of F68-HA-DOX is irregular nanoparticle with an average diameter of 55 nm (Fig. 3C), which is secured through hydrophilic and hydrophobic effects. PDAD-doping assemblies (PDAD@F68-HA-DOX) have the similar shape as F68-HA-DOX, and PDADs with a relatively high contrast could be obviously observed on the surface of assemblies, indicating that the fluorescent PDADs have been loaded on the nanoprobes successfully (Fig. 3D). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was conducted to evaluate the size distributions and zeta potentials of various NPs. As illustrated in Fig. 4A, the diameters of PDADs, F199-HA-DOX, and PDAD@F68-HA-DOX are 7, 60, and 70 nm, respectively, nearly consistent with their TEM images. And their zeta potentials are displayed in Fig. 4B. Due to the presence of secondary amine, PDADs could present positive charge (+4 mV). However, anion polymers HA would make the F68-HA-DOX assemblies exhibit a strong electronegativity (about −35 mV). For the resultant NPs obtained through combination of PDADs with F68-HA-DOX, the charge of PDAD@F68-HA-DOX is close to neutral (−6 mV), attributed to the fact that PDADs could partly neutralize the negative charge of F68-HA-DOX. The changing tendency of diameters and zeta potentials are consistent with the addition order of reagents, demonstrating the successful fabrication of nanodrugs. To further research the composition of nanodrugs, UV–vis absorption spectra of different substances were measured. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the appearance of two characteristic absorption peaks assigned to DOX and PDADs in the absorption spectra of PDAD@F68-HA-DOX NPs reveals that both PDADs and DOX have been loaded on the nanomaterials. Combination with above characterization data, we could conclude that PDAD@F68-HA-DOX nanoprobes have been synthesized as our design, and all the reagents have been successfully integrated into NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction of FRET system for HAase detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quantum yields of PDADs and DOX is 13.78 % and 10.11 %, respectively, determined by a steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer. To verify the possibility to construct FRET system, the fluorescence emission spectrum of PDADs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX were scanned and integrated into the same spectrum. As shown in Fig. 5 C, with the excitation wavelength at 405 nm, the emission spectrum of PDADs shows the maximum fluorescence intensity at about 475 nm. Meanwhile, the maximum absorption appears at around 480 nm in the absorption spectrum of DOX. This perfect coincidence between the fluorescence emission spectrum of donors and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of acceptors provides the rational fundament to construct an efficient FRET system.

Subsequently, different amounts of HA-DOX were added into PDAD solutions to confirm the FRET process in the nanoprobes through recording the fluorescence changes of above solutions. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, the initial PDAD solution could emit intense blue fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light. While with the addition of HA-DOX into PDAD solutions, distinct attenuation of blue fluorescence accompanied by the enhancement of red fluorescence could be observed, whose amplitude was facilitated by the increased amount of HA-DOX. Their fluorescence spectra also validate above results (Fig. 5B). With the increased concentration of HA-DOX, the fluorescence intensity of PDADs at 475 nm could continuously decrease, while that of DOX at about 600 nm would gradually increase, indicating the FRET process between PDADs and DOX.

HAase activity was evaluated in vitro through monitoring the fluorescence changes of PDAD@F68-HA-DOX solutions with the addition of different amounts of HAase. As displayed in Fig. 5D, E, the continuous addition of HAase leads to gradual decrease of the fluorescence intensity at 600 nm and concurrently drastic enhancement at 475 nm. In addition, the ratio of emission peak intensity (I475/I600) and HAase activity (in the range of 0.125–16 U/mL) presents a good linear relationship. As shown in Fig. 5F, the linear equation could be described as follow: ratio (I475/I600) = 0.9409 + 1.0927[HAase]. The linear correlation coefficient is as high as 0.996, and the limit of detection (LOD) could be calculated to be 0.04 U/mL. It’s reported that the HAase level was 0.2−0.45 U/mL in normal tissues, and more than 10s-fold higher in tumor tissues [14,40]. Therefore, this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be used to detect the activity of intracellular HAase. Compared with other methods for HAase detection, as illustrated in Table S1, this nanoprobe with highly efficient FRET pairs exhibits an outstanding performance in the determination of endogenous HAase.

The peculiarity that a ratiometric sensor could effectively eliminate various kinds of interferences is completely necessary for the steady and accurate detection. In order to evaluate the selectivity of this nanoprobe, various species (including NaCl, KCl, MgSO4, ascorbic acid, glucose, GSH, cysteine, BSA, ALP, lysozyme, and trypsin) were tested under the same conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S4, only HAase could induce a considerable change of I475/I600 ratio, and the addition of other substances hardly caused fluorescence changes, exhibiting a good selectivity of the nanoprobe to HAase. More importantly, the anti-interference property of the nanoprobe in the presence of various species was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the additions of different potential interferents hardly affect the detection results, exhibiting an eminent anti-interference performance of nanoprobe. In addition, the effects of pH and ionic concentration on the fluorescence of sensor system were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, fluorescence signal of the nanosensor system could be invariable with different pH values or ionic concentrations. These results reveal that PDAD@F68-HA-DOX nanoprobe has an outstanding selectivity to HAase and a strong anti-interference ability, which is crucial for improvement of the detection accuracy.

With the excellent selectivity and sensitivity to HAase, the prepared PDAD@F68-HA-DOX nanosensor system was utilized to image the endogenous HAase to further expand its potential application in biological systems. HeLa (tumor) and NIH-3T3 (normal) cells were seeded into the confocal dishes and cultured with medium containing PDAD@F68-HA-DOX (10 μg/mL) for 2 h, and then monitored through a CLSM. As displayed in Fig. 6 , intense fluorescence (Iblue) can be detected from the blue channel of HeLa cells. Meanwhile, its red fluorescence signal (Ired) is extremely weak. However, HeLa cells pretreated with HA exhibited distinctly different results. Considerable red fluorescence signal (Ired) from HA-pretreated HeLa cells could be detected. Simultaneously, a drastically weakened signal from the blue fluorescence channel (Iblue) occurs. Similar images could be also captured from NIH-3T3 cells. These experimental phenomena are the consequences of the varied levels of HAase in different cells. HeLa cells with overexpressed HAase could lead to the decomposition of HA and further collapse of FRET nanoprobe. The release of receptors would effectively remove the quenching effect on donors, so blue fluorescence of PDADs would recover, and the released DOX without energy source could not emit its red fluorescence. As for the pretreated HeLa cells, HAase in the tumor cells was depleted through the addition of excess HA and failed to destroy the FRET-based nanoprobe. So the nanoprobe would still emit the red fluorescence of DOX. As a normal cell, NIH-3T3 cells could not provide sufficient HAase, so they should show similar phenomenon to that of the pretreated HeLa cells. Therefore, it could be clearly found that the value of Iblue/Ired is related to the endogenous HAase activity. Based on the ratio of fluorescence intensities in different channels, HAase activity can be easily estimated in different cells. Meanwhile, since PDAD@F68-HA-DOX can specifically distinguish tumor cells from normal cells through labels of different fluorescence colors, this theranostic nanoprobe would help medical personnel to perform accurate intraoperative tumor resection. Moreover, the release of anticancer drugs (DOX) triggered by overexpressed HAase in tumor cells could also play a role in the subsequent cancer treatment.

The in situ released chemotherapeutic drugs would induce cytotoxicity to tumor cells, so cellular viability assays were conducted by incubating HeLa or NIH-3T3 cells in culture medium containing different concentrations of various drugs. Compared with PDAD@F68-HA, PDAD@ F68-HA-DOX could cause greater toxicity to HeLa cells, which was comparable to that of pure DOX at the same concentration (Fig. 7 A), implying that almost all DOX loaded on NPs was released and could lead damage to tumor cells. However, these theranostic nanoprobes exhibited notably different cytotoxicity to normal cells. As displayed in Fig. 7B, pure DOX could cause intense damage to NIH-3T3 cells (viability < 20 % at the concentration of 1.6 μg/mL), while the cytotoxicity induced by PDAD@F68-HA-DOX was much weaker (viability ≈ 60 %). This could be owing to the fact that the content of HAase in normal cells is insufficient to induce the decomposition of theranostic NPs, so the DOX loaded on nanodrugs would not be released. Cytotoxicity experiments manifest that these HAase-activated nanoprobes have a remarkable potential in achieving accurate cancer treatment with few side effects.

In this work, a FRET-based theranostic nanoprobe (PDAD@F68-HA-DOX) has been established through self-assembly for HAase detection and cancer therapy. Herein, HA is not only utilized to sustain the framework of nanodrugs, but also employed as the substrate of bio-enzyme to be determinated. With the aid of efficient FRET-paired fluorophores, this theranostic nanoprobe activated by the stimulus of HAase could convert the emitted fluorescence color from red to blue and simultaneously release the loaded anticancer drugs. Base on the changed fluorescence color and intensity, HAase activity could be sensitively and accurately evaluated. The chemotherapeutic drug released in the lesion area could induce significant damage to the tumor cells. Compared with other methods of HAase detection, this nanosensor system could be easily prepared and implemented, and the ratiometric test mechanism also endows this nanoprobe with a higher reliability and accuracy. In summary, this nanotheranostic system with a high FRET efficiency has provided a convenient and reliable approach for HAase detection and cancer therapy.

Yazhou Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Hengzhi Zhao: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Na Gao: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Chunlei Yang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Rubo Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Xiaoling Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 21275018 and 21575015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130201.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colon Cancer sidedness, presentation, and survival at different stages

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.B. Ulanja, M. Rishi, B.D. Beutler, M. Sharma, D.R. Patterson, N. Gullapalli, et al., Colon Cancer Sidedness, Presentation, and Survival at Different Stages, J Oncol, 2019(2019) 4315032.

Dekker, N.M. deSouza, et al., Characterisation and classification of oligometastatic disease: a European Society for Radiotherapy and Oncology and European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer consensus recommendation, The Lancet Oncology, 21(2020) e18-e28.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New insights into designing hybrid nanoparticles for lung cancer: diagnosis and treatment

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biosensors for early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, X. Li, Y. Song, L. Li, W. Shi, H. Ma, An Upconversion Luminescence Nanoprobe for the Ultrasensitive Detection of Hyaluronidase, Anal Chem, 87(2015) 5816-5823.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Liu, X. Yan, Q. Lai, X. Su, Bimetallic gold/silver nanoclusters-gold nanoparticles based fluorescent sensing platform via the inner filter effect for hyaluronidase activity detection, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 282(2019) 45-51.

Gao, et al., Turn-on theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe by electrostatic self-assembly of carbon dots with doxorubicin for targeted cancer cell imaging, in vivo hyaluronidase analysis, and targeted drug delivery, Biosens Bioelectron, 96(2017) 300-307.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, S. Wang, L. Xu, L. Feng, Y. Ji, L. Tao, et al., Biocompatible polydopamine fluorescent organic nanoparticles: facile preparation and cell imaging, Nanoscale, 4(2012) 5581-5584.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Yildirim, M. Bayindir, Turn-on fluorescent dopamine sensing based on in situ formation of visible light emitting polydopamine nanoparticles, Anal Chem, 86(2014) 5508-5512.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Yang, S. Zhang, X. Chen, X. Liu, Z. Wang, Y. Li, Recent developments in polydopamine fluorescent nanomaterials, Materials Horizons, 7(2020) 746-761.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tumor microenvironment-responsive theranostic nanoplatform for in situ self-boosting combined phototherapy through intracellular reassembly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, J. Jing, F. Jia, S. Su, Y. Tian, N. Gao, et al., Tumor Microenvironment-Responsive Theranostic Nanoplatform for in Situ Self-Boosting Combined Phototherapy through Intracellular Reassembly, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces, 12(2020) 6966-6977.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Tang, S. Mo, S.G. Liu, L.L. Liao, N.B. Li, H.Q. Luo, Synthesis of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles by Michael addition reaction as an analysis platform to detect iron ions and pyrophosphate efficiently and construction of an IMPLICATION logic gate, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 255(2018) 754-762.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cationic carbon dots for modification-free detection of hyaluronidase via an electrostatic-controlled ratiometric fluorescence assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Yang, J. Ni, F. Luo, W. Weng, Q. Wei, Z. Lin, et al., Cationic Carbon Dots for Modification-Free Detection of Hyaluronidase via an Electrostatic-Controlled Ratiometric Fluorescence Assay, Anal Chem, 89(2017) 8384-8390.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yazhou Liu is a Ph.D. student in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology. His main research field is the construction of theranostic nanoplatform.
Hengzhi Zhao is a Ph.D. student in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology. His current research is focused on DNA-based biosensing and drug delivery.
Na Gao is a Ph.D. student in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology. Her research interest focuses on the synthesis and application of fluorescence nanoprobes.
Chunlei Yang is a Ph.D. student in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology. Her research interest focuses on the fluorescence nanoprobe and fluorescence imaging.
Rubo Zhang is an associate professor in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology. His current research focuses on design and synthesis of fluorescent probes.

Xiaoling Zhang is a professor in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology. Her research interest focuses on molecular recognition and chemical and biological sensors.
